policy_statement: Increase hospital and ICU capacity and improve healthcare access in medically underserved areas.
mechanism_description: Expand healthcare infrastructure and access so the health system can better withstand and respond to climate-driven illness and extreme-event surges.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard + health stressors → ↑ healthcare demand/surge risk → ↑ capacity & access → ↓ vulnerability (morbidity/mortality, service disruption)
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Increase healthcare infrastructure and operational capacity (e.g., hospital beds/ICU capacity) and expand access in underserved areas.  
(2) Process: Greater surge capacity, staffing/throughput, and earlier/more equitable access to care improves the health system’s ability to absorb and respond to spikes in climate-related illness/injury (heat stress, respiratory events from smoke/dust, flood trauma) and maintain continuity of services during disruptions.  
(3) Outcome: Reduced climate-related health impacts (fewer severe cases/deaths) and improved community resilience to extreme events.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is lowering morbidity and mortality during climate-driven extreme events by increasing healthcare system surge capacity and access.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: climate hazards increase acute health burdens; expanded healthcare capacity/access reduces exposure-to-impact translation by enabling timely treatment and surge response—an adaptation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while healthcare facilities may change energy use, the mechanism described is not about reducing energy/GHGs but about coping capacity and health outcomes under climate stress.  
3) Inherent secondaries: none are unavoidable—any mitigation/resource-efficiency benefits (or increased emissions from expanded facilities) are contingent on design/operations, not intrinsic to “capacity expansion.”  
4) Edge cases applied: if paired with low-carbon hospital retrofits or onsite renewables, those would be separate mitigation mechanisms; not assumed here.  
5) Label applies to all instances: any policy that expands healthcare capacity/access to handle climate-driven health surges functions primarily as adaptation by improving resilience of health services and reducing climate-related harm.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Healthcare capacity expansion can increase operational emissions (more floor area/energy use) unless coupled with efficiency/clean energy measures; however, that is not inherent to the stated mechanism and should be classified separately if explicitly included.
additional_secondary_evidence: Public health (heat & air quality): Las Vegas faces extreme heat and dust storms, and increasing hospital and ICU capacity plus improving healthcare access directly reduces exposure/sensitivity because it increases surge capacity and timely treatment for heat illness and respiratory impacts during high-heat and poor-air-quality episodes.
key_indicators: “Increasing hospital and ICU capacity”; “health care access”; “medically underserved areas”
co_benefits: Health equity improvements; improved routine healthcare outcomes; economic resilience via reduced lost work/health costs
instance_edge_case_notes: If implemented primarily via insurance/coverage expansion or service-delivery programs rather than facility buildout, the instrument_type could shift toward “incentive-program” or “planning-strategy”; the text as written is most consistent with capacity expansion (infrastructure investment).
